text: This is Dr. Rani Abaza. This is a robotic prostatectomy with lymph node dissection. We do it as a transperitoneal operation with a total of five incisions.

The surgeon operates with a cautery scissor in the right hand, a fenestrated Maryland bipolar in the left hand, and a prograsp in the fourth robotic arm. The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg, and the camera port is inserted at the umbilicus for a clear view of the surgical field.
An eight millimeter air seal assistant port is visible, used for air seal insufflation and passing instruments like needles and specimen bags, while the surgeon manages suction independently.

The surgeon releases the bowel and performs a posterior dissection of the seminal vesicles. An initial incision is made approximately one centimeter above the line between the whitish peritoneal reflection on the bladder and the yellowish perirectal fat.

After completing the medial dissection, the surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to access the contralateral shoulder, providing a view of the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicles. The seminal vesicles are dissected out, appearing clean with no residual tissue attached.

The surgeon uses monopolar and bipolar cautery at a setting of twenty-five to minimize thermal spread and potential nerve injury. The posterior dissection approach is used to expose the vas deferens. The vas is then grasped with the fourth arm and lifted straight up for further manipulation.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to actively provide exposure, revealing the tip of the seminal vesicle. The camera angle shifts to the contralateral shoulder, displaying the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle. The surgeon carefully maneuvers to stay directly on top of the seminal vesicle, ensuring precise dissection.

The surgeon cuts the vas deferens in line with the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle, allowing both ends to retract. The incision is made to the right of the ligament, extending down to reveal the pubic bone. A natural plane is identified, facilitating blunt dissection.

The surgeon connects the dissection points, ensuring alignment by visualizing the pubic bone to avoid moving too anteriorly into the rectus. Fat is being cleaned from the prostate using cautery set at 40-40 in preparation for node dissection.

The surgeon uses cautery set at forty-forty to drop the bladder and perform nodal dissection, utilizing a pre-developed plane between the perivascular fat and nodes. The obturator nerve is visible, and the surgeon carefully dissects around the vein, removing surrounding tissue.

The surgeon applies clips to the lymphatics returning from the leg, sealing around the entire packet. Clips are placed both above and below the vein, and bipolar cautery is used to seal the tissue between the pelvic side wall muscle and the vein. The surgeon inspects the area to ensure no lymphatics are left unsealed during the dissection beneath the vein.

The surgeon performs bladder neck dissection, using cautery set to twenty-five to minimize tissue charring. An incision is made over the prostate, visible at the top of the screen, ensuring it does not penetrate into the prostate tissue.

The surgeon makes an incision and uses the left hand to apply constant backwards pressure on the bladder neck, carefully peeling the prostate and bladder apart. The cautery is used gently to touch the tissue without digging into it. Upon reaching the urethra, the surgeon uses the fourth arm to lift it towards the anterior abdominal wall, revealing the posterior bladder neck.

The surgeon begins the incision at the corners of the bladder neck, carefully releasing the corners to maintain a tight bladder neck. The size of the bladder neck is visibly fixed, preventing it from enlarging further.

The surgeon uses a low cautery setting to minimize charring while dissecting around the bladder neck fibers. The left hand provides stretch, clearly revealing the bladder neck fibers and ensuring the dissection remains away from the prostate.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to dissect the lateral corners of the prostate, removing bladder muscle fibers to expose underlying fat. The bladder is positioned low, avoiding obstruction, while the rectum is visible in the background as rectal dissection begins.

The surgeon makes an incision in the posterior Denonvilliers' fascia, entering the perirectal fat plane and peeling the perirectal fat from the backside of the prostate. The high anterior release of the prostatic fascia is performed to closely follow the prostate and identify the neurovascular bundle. The air seal system maintains constant pneumoperitoneum, reducing venous bleeding during suction.

The surgeon uses a robotic clip applier to secure the pedicle near the prostate, placing the clip high to avoid injuring the neurovascular bundle. The procedure is conducted at a pneumo pressure of six.

The surgeon demonstrates the proximity of the pedicle to the neurovascular bundle at the six o'clock position on the screen. The neurovascular bundle is seen turning and moving posterolaterally to the prostate, where it has been peeled away. A small incision is made at the edge of the non-VAs, leaving a square of the non-VAs on the posterior aspect of the gland.

The surgeon maintains a pneumo pressure of six with minimal venous bleeding while using suction to clear smoke. The high anterior release on the prostatic fascia is visible. The surgeon manipulates the pedicle, pushing it aside to visually locate the bottom edge of the prostate as it turns.

The surgeon performs nerve-sparing dissection, aiming to preserve the neurovascular bundle while cutting the Denonvier's edge medially to separate it from the prostate capsule. The dissection extends to the apex of the gland.

The surgeon cuts through the dorsal venous complex (DVC) and oversews it, minimizing bleeding due to the low pneumo pressure of six. The urethra is visible, and the catheter is reinserted as the surgeon proceeds to cut the urethra.

The surgeon pulls the catheter back to expose the posterior aspect of the urethra, preparing for the final steps of the prostatectomy. The prostatectomy specimen is visible, showing successful nerve-sparing techniques, with attention to the capsule of the prostate posterolateral and the base adjacent to the seminal vesicles where the pedicle was located.

The surgeon places a DVC stitch using an ovicryl suture and a CT-I needle, performing a figure-of-eight technique to secure the stitch. The pedicle is taken close to the prostate, maximizing the preservation of the neurovascular bundles. The stitch is then anchored through the pubic symphysis.

The surgeon compresses the DVC against the underside of the bone to prevent postoperative bleeding. Using an ovacrol suture on a CT-1 needle, they perform a Rocco stitch for posterior reconstruction, horizontally passing under the urethra to secure the posterior plate. The cut edge of the non-BAs and the peritoneal edge near the seminal vesicles are included, ensuring the posterior bladder is positioned away from the bladder neck to avoid interference with the anastomosis. A slider knot is used to secure the structures, preventing bleeding that could disrupt the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a Stratafix barbed suture to perform the anastomosis, starting at the four or five o'clock position. Two stitches are placed through the urethra and three through the bladder, cinching them down to complete the posterior aspect of the anastomosis. The mucosa is carefully aligned to prevent leaks and avoid the need for a drain.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and inserts a catheter into the urethra. The bladder is filled with saline to check for any leaks at the anastomosis site. No leakage is observed, indicating a successful closure.
